question: A patient presents to the emergency department with sudden onset focal neurologic deficits and decreased level of consciousness. Noncontrast head imaging is obtained to evaluate the cause. Based on the imaging, which of the following symptoms or signs would be most likely?

options.D: Sudden severe headache with neck stiffness

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the noncontrast head CT for lesion location and mass effect. The image shows a large hypodense region of the right cerebral hemisphere affecting the lateral convexity with loss of normal sulcal markings on that side, and notable mass effect with shift of midline structures toward the left. There is asymmetry of the ventricular system consistent with secondary mass effect.

Next, I relate those imaging findings to the clinical question, which asks which symptom or sign is most likely. A focal destructive lesion in the right cerebral hemisphere (particularly involving the motor cortex/adjacent subcortical structures supplied by the right MCA territory) will produce contralateral motor deficits. The image’s right-sided infarct with mass effect therefore predicts weakness on the left side of the body.

Then I consider the other options briefly in light of the image: right-sided hemiparesis would be expected from a left hemispheric lesion, not the right-sided lesion seen here; global aphasia typically requires dominant-hemisphere involvement (not supported by a right-sided lesion); sudden severe headache with neck stiffness suggests subarachnoid hemorrhage rather than the large right hemispheric infarct pictured; and a left homonymous hemianopia would require specific involvement of the right visual pathways/occipital cortex, which is not the dominant finding here.

Therefore, the imaging findings of a large right hemispheric infarct with mass effect most directly explain contralateral (left-sided) hemiparesis.
caption: Case 1 Noncontrast CT scan of the head with a large acute right middle cerebral artery (MCA) territory infarct (small arrows), midline shift resulting in indentation of the contralateral cerebral crus by the tentorium cerebelli (large arrow), and left sided unilateral, noncommunicating hydrocephalus (arrowheads).
sub_caption: Noncontrast CT scan of the head with a large acute right middle cerebral artery (MCA) territory infarct (small arrows), midline shift resulting in indentation of the contralateral cerebral crus by the tentorium cerebelli (large arrow), and left sided unilateral, noncommunicating hydrocephalus (arrowheads).
image_context: A 59-year old male with hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and atrial fibrillation presented to an outside hospital with sudden onset of slurred speech, left hemiparalysis, and right gaze preference. CT head revealed a large acute right middle cerebral artery territory infact with 3 mm of leftward midline shift. Patient was transferred to a tertiary care facility and experienced declining status requiring intubation with inability to follow commands and physical exam limited to reflexes. Repeat CT scan of the head revealed progressive leftward midline shift (up to 16 mm), indentation of the contralateral cerebral crus by the tentorium cerebelli, and left sided unilateral, noncommunicating hydrocephalus (<xref rid="gr1" ref-type="fig">Fig. 1</xref>). An emergent right hemicraniectomy was performed with little improvement in patient status. A subsequent MRI of the head revealed diffusion restriction and fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) hyperintensity diffusely involving the left SCA territory, right posterior cerebral artery (PCA) territory, and within the left lateral mesencephalon (including the crus cerebri, lateral tegmentum, and upper tectum) (). An emergent right hemicraniectomy was performed with little improvement in patient status. A subsequent MRI of the head revealed diffusion restriction and fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) hyperintensity diffusely involving the left SCA territory, right posterior cerebral artery (PCA) territory, and within the left lateral mesencephalon (including the crus cerebri, lateral tegmentum, and upper tectum) (<xref rid="gr2" ref-type="fig">Fig. 2</xref>A-E). A tiny acute hematoma was also present within the left lateral tegmentum (A-E). A tiny acute hematoma was also present within the left lateral tegmentum (<xref rid="gr2" ref-type="fig">Fig. 2</xref>B-C). The patient made little progress in the ensuing days and expired shortly after being switched to comfort measures only.B-C). The patient made little progress in the ensuing days and expired shortly after being switched to comfort measures only.Fig. 1Case 1 Noncontrast CT scan of the head with a large acute right middle cerebral artery (MCA) territory infarct (small arrows), midline shift resulting in indentation of the contralateral cerebral crus by the tentorium cerebelli (large arrow), and left sided unilateral, noncommunicating hydrocephalus (arrowheads).Fig. 1Fig. 2Case 1 MRI of the head post right hemicraniectomy with evolving subacute right MCA territory infarct, new ipsilateral PCA territory infarct, and new contralateral SCA territory infarct and Kernohan notch phenomenon type mesencephalic injury. (A) Coronal T2 image demonstrating large evolving subacute right MCA territory infarct with mild petechial type hemorrhagic conversion (small arrows) and T2 hyperintense signal abnormality within the left crus cerebri and lateral tegmentum (big arrows) suggestive of Kernohan notch phenomenon type injury. (B) Axial FLAIR image demonstrating abnormal FLAIR hyperintense signal within the left crus cerebri, lateral tegmentum, and upper tectum (big arrows) and superimposed FLAIR hypointense hemorrhage within the left upper tectum suggestive of Kernohan notch phenomenon type injury. (C) Axial diffusion weighted Imaging (DWI) image demonstrating high DWI signal within the left crus cerebri, lateral tegmentum, and upper tectum (big arrows), right PCA territory (arrowheads), and left SCA territory (small arrows). (D) Axial DWI image demonstrating high DWI signal within the left crus cerebri, lateral tegmentum, and upper tectum (big arrows), right PCA territory (arrowheads), and left SCA territory (small arrows).(E) Axial apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) image demonstrating low ADC signal, consistent with diffusion restriction, within the left lateral mesencephalon (big arrows), right PCA territory (arrowheads), and left SCA territory (small arrows). PCA, posterior cerebral artery; SCA, superior cerebellar artery.Fig. 2
